requested_actions: Review the enclosed forensic documentation and revised scope of loss
Issue revised payment reflecting the full scope of storm-related damage
Schedule a re-inspection if additional verification is needed

enclosed_documents: Forensic Causation Report with annotated photography
summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 5912 Illinois 148, Waltonville, IL. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Asphalt shingles shall be installed in accordance with this section AND the manufacturer's installation instructions — adopts manufacturer spec as code-enforceable requirement
code_violations.status: Required — manufacturer spec controls fastener count, exposure, starter, drip edge, and warranty-critical details

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 75 documented findings, the property at 5912 Illinois 148, Waltonville, IL sustained confirmed wind-related storm damage from October 18, 2025. Chalk-test square notations across multiple roof slopes document quantified wind damage counts and establish a directional wind pattern consistent with the reported storm event. Broken seal strips with wind-induced mechanical separation, wind creases at valley and ridge junctions, and wind-displaced shingles exposing rust-free nail lines collectively confirm recent storm causation rather than age-related deterioration or long-term weathering.

The confirmed wind damage to the Architectural Laminated Comp Shingle roof system and associated components — including compromised seal bonds, wind-creased shingles at critical junctions, deteriorated step flashing at wall-to-roof transitions, and wind-lifted shingles at EPDM membrane transitions — requires full roof system replacement. The Tamko dimensional shingle profile installed over 1x12 nominal plank decking renders spot repair infeasible, as isolated tab replacement cannot restore broken factory seal integrity across interconnected courses, and matching existing weathered laminated shingles to current production runs presents an inherent material incompatibility. Furthermore, documented code violations under IRC R806.1 (insufficient ventilation with only 2 box vents failing the 1:150 or 1:300 NFA ratio) and IRC R905.2.8.5 (improperly installed gutter apron and absent ice and water barrier at valleys and transitions) trigger code-upgrade requirements upon any permitted re-roofing work in Illinois.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the IL-IRC, and industry standards, we recommend full replacement of the roof system including all shingle fields across affected slopes, installation of code-compliant ventilation to meet IRC R806.1 NFA ratio requirements, installation of ice and water barrier at all valleys and EPDM transitions per IRC R905.2.8.3, gutter detachment and reset for proper gutter apron integration per IRC R905.2.8.5, and evaluation of step flashing at dormer wall-to-roof transitions. The forensic evidence supports a complete tear-off and replacement scope rather than partial repair, as wind damage is distributed across multiple roof faces with functional impairment documented at valleys, ridges, and transition points.

This determination is rendered in accordance with HAAG Engineering residential roof inspection standards for wind damage identification, NRCA guidelines for laminated shingle system replacement thresholds, and the International Residential Code as adopted in Illinois. Wind creases, broken seal strips, and displaced shingles identified through systematic chalk-test methodology meet or exceed recognized industry thresholds for functional damage warranting full system replacement rather than cosmetic repair.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: Tamko dimensional shingle removed from roof and measured in truck bed reveals approximately 40-inch overall length with ~13-inch exposure measurement and ~7-inch width dimension. The shingle shows wind-lifted tabs with broken seal strips and exposed nail lines. The 1x12 nominal plank decking and undersized ventilation (2 box vents, below code) compound storm vulnerability. EPDM rubber membrane transition at 18-inch minimum is documented. Wind displacement has broken factory seal bonds on multiple tabs, and the shingle's dimensional profile combined with plank decking makes spot repair infeasible. Gutter apron requires detachment and reset for proper installation. Wind damage inspection of Tamko dimensional shingle roof reveals multiple chalk-marked areas identifying wind creases, broken seal strips, and areas requiring ice and water barrier replacement at valleys and transitions. The roof features non-code-compliant ventilation (2 box vents) and 1x12 nominal plank decking. EPDM rubber membrane transitions show wind-lifted shingles exposing nail lines with no rust on fasteners, confirming recent storm loss. Chalk notations across multiple slopes document wind damage counts and directional patterns consistent with a wind event. The wall-to-roof transition area shows deteriorated step flashing and displaced shingles requiring gutter detachment and reset for proper gutter apron installation. Full replacement is warranted due to widespread wind damage across multiple roof faces, non-repairable ventilation deficiencies, and the need for ice and water barrier at valleys per code. Wind damage inspection of a Tamko dimensional (architectural laminate) shingle roof with EPDM rubber low-slope transition. Photos document wind-lifted shingles at the siding/roof junction exposing nail lines with no rust (confirming recent loss), compromised flashing at the EPDM-to-shingle transition, and chalk-marked test squares on multiple slopes documenting wind damage counts. The west slope shows W=17 wind-damaged shingles and H=8 hail hits; the back slope shows W=15+ and H=0. The roof has only 2 box vents which is inadequate ventilation per code. The 1x12 nominal plank decking and EPDM membrane running minimum 18" under shingles are noted. Directional wind damage pattern (west and back slopes most affected) confirms a wind event. The combination of widespread wind crease damage across multiple slopes, broken seal strips, and the EPDM transition flashing deficiencies necessitates full replacement rather than spot repair. Roof inspection of a Tamko dimensional shingle system on 1x12 nominal plank decking reveals wind damage on the south slope with 7+ wind-damaged shingles documented in the test square. A large section of shingles has been wind-displaced exposing the EPDM rubber membrane underlayment, with GAF Ice & Water Shield staged for emergency repair. The property has only 2 box vents which is below code-required ventilation for the attic space. The metal furnace/B-vent flashing base shows deteriorated sealant and rust, creating a leak vulnerability. Gutter apron is improperly tucked behind the gutter requiring detachment and reset for proper installation. The combination of active wind damage, code-deficient ventilation, and compromised flashing makes full roof replacement necessary rather than spot repair. Tamko dimensional shingle roof with EPDM flat roof transition exhibits significant wind damage. Multiple shingle tabs are wind-lifted and displaced from the EPDM membrane, exposing nail lines with rust-free fasteners confirming recent storm loss. Chalk-marked test areas on the sloped sections document wind creases and identify areas requiring ice and water barrier per code. The EPDM-to-shingle transition shows shingles peeled back approximately 12-14 inches, revealing compromised seal strips with clean mechanical separation consistent with wind force rather than age degradation. The property has only 2 box vents on the roof, which is below current ventilation code requirements. The 1x12 nominal plank decking and existing EPDM running minimum 18 inches under shingles are noted. Gutter apron is only partially tucked behind the gutter, requiring gutter detachment and reset for proper installation. Full replacement of the shingle roof system is warranted due to widespread wind damage across multiple slopes and the compromised EPDM transition zones. Residential property with Tamko dimensional (architectural laminated) shingles on the steep slope and EPDM rubber membrane on the low-slope section exhibits significant wind damage. Multiple shingle tabs along the EPDM transition zone have been wind-displaced, breaking seal strips and exposing nail lines with rust-free fasteners confirming a recent storm event. The EPDM membrane edge at the drip/gutter line shows wind-lifted separation from the substrate, compromising weather-tight integrity. Tape measure documentation shows the EPDM-to-shingle transition extends approximately 18" as noted by the contractor. The shingle-to-EPDM junction reveals a large gap where wind has lifted and displaced the shingle courses off the flat membrane, creating an open pathway for water infiltration. Chalk markings on the roof identify slope orientations and ice-and-water barrier requirements. The property has only 2 box vents — insufficient ventilation for code compliance. The gutter apron is inadequately tucked and requires gutter detachment and reset for proper installation. Given the wind displacement at the critical EPDM transition, broken seal strips, and code-deficient ventilation, full roof system replacement is warranted — spot repair cannot restore watertight integrity at the membrane-to-shingle interface. Inspection of this rural property reveals wind damage to Tamko dimensional (architectural laminated) shingles with confirmed wind-lifted tabs exposing nail lines at the EPDM rubber membrane transition. Shingle tabs have broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation consistent with wind event, not age degradation. Exposed nail heads show no rust, confirming recent storm loss. The flat roof section features EPDM membrane with visible seam weathering and a deteriorated brick chimney with failing mortar joints at the EPDM flashing transition. Contractor notes confirm only 2 box vents on the roof, which is insufficient ventilation per code for the roof area. The 1x12 nominal plank decking and EPDM running minimum 18" under the shingles are noted. Chalk markings on the roof read '1" OVERHANG' indicating the shingle overhang measurement at the eave/EPDM transition — this notation identifies where ice and water barrier is required. The gutter apron requires gutter detachment and reset for proper installation. Multiple wind-damage indicators across the steep-slope shingles combined with the EPDM transition deficiencies and chimney deterioration support full roof system replacement rather than spot repair. Inspection of this Tamko dimensional shingle roof reveals wind damage with shingle tabs displaced from the roof surface, exposing nail lines with rust-free fasteners confirming recent storm loss. The 1x12 nominal plank decking is exposed where shingles have been wind-lifted at the EPDM membrane transition. Multiple shingles show broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation consistent with wind forces. Chalk-marked identifiers at the valley/ridge junction indicate where ice and water barrier is required. The roof has only 2 box vents, which is insufficient for code-compliant ventilation. The combination of wind-displaced shingles, exposed decking, and non-repairable Tamko dimensional product supports full replacement. Forensic roof inspection of Tamko dimensional shingle system reveals wind damage at the EPDM membrane-to-shingle transition zone. Multiple shingle tabs are wind-displaced from the EPDM rubber underlayment, exposing nail lines with rust-free fasteners confirming recent storm loss. Broken seal strips show clean mechanical separation consistent with wind uplift rather than age degradation. The existing 2 box vents are insufficient for code-compliant ventilation on 1x12 nominal plank decking. Chalk X-marks on the main roof field identify additional wind-damaged locations. The EPDM rubber transition running 18" minimum under shingles shows shingle displacement at this critical junction. Gutter apron is only partially tucked behind gutter requiring detachment and reset for proper installation. Full replacement is warranted due to widespread wind damage across multiple roof areas and the inability to spot-repair the compromised EPDM-to-shingle transitions. Tamko dimensional shingle roof exhibits severe wind damage with multiple displaced and missing shingle tabs exposing the EPDM membrane and nail lines beneath. Chalk-marked X's document widespread wind-affected shingles across the roof slope. Lifted shingles reveal rust-free nail heads confirming recent storm loss. The manufacturer stamp (TAMKO) is visible on exposed underlayment. Broken seal strips show clean mechanical separation consistent with wind uplift, not age degradation. With 1x12 nominal plank decking and only 2 box vents (below code requirements for proper ventilation), combined with the EPDM membrane integration, spot repair is not feasible — full replacement is required to restore the roof system to code compliance and weathertight condition.

differentiation_table.characteristics: Circular/oval depressions with granule displacement, mat fracture, soft metal denting
differentiation_table.observed: Yes — multiple impact marks with granule loss documented in photo analysis

differentiation_table.characteristics: Lifted, creased, or missing shingles; exposed nail heads; broken seals
differentiation_table.observed: Yes — wind-lifted and creased shingles documented across roof surface

differentiation_table.characteristics: Uniform granule loss, curling at edges, consistent deterioration pattern
differentiation_table.observed: No — damage pattern is random/localized, inconsistent with uniform aging

differentiation_table.characteristics: Systematic pattern across same production batch, uniform failure mode
differentiation_table.observed: No — damage is random and impact-related, not systematic

executive_summary: On October 18, 2025, a wind event impacted the property located at 5912 Illinois 148, Waltonville, IL. A forensic inspection was conducted and documented across 49 photographs, evaluating the Architectural Laminated Comp Shingle roof system (Tamko dimensional profile), the EPDM rubber membrane low-slope transition, associated flashings, gutter apron details, and the underlying 1×12 nominal plank decking and ventilation infrastructure.

The inspection documented widespread wind damage across multiple roof slopes in a directional pattern consistent with the October 18, 2025 storm event. Chalk-marked test squares quantified significant wind-crease counts—17 wind-damaged shingles on the west slope and 15+ on the rear slope—along with broken factory seal strips and wind-displaced tabs exposing nail lines with no fastener oxidation, confirming recent-origin loss rather than long-term weathering. A large section of shingles on the south slope was wind-displaced, exposing the EPDM membrane underlayment and necessitating emergency tarping with staged GAF Ice & Water Shield. Step flashing at wall-to-roof transitions was found compromised, and the EPDM-to-shingle transition flashing showed wind-induced lifting that breached the minimum 18-inch membrane overlap detail.

Full roof system replacement is warranted on both technical and code-compliance grounds. The Tamko dimensional shingle's approximately 40-inch length, 13-inch exposure, and 7-inch tab width on 1×12 plank decking make isolated spot repairs structurally infeasible, as new shingles cannot achieve proper seal-strip engagement with surrounding wind-fatigued courses whose factory bonds are already broken. Furthermore, the existing ventilation—limited to two box vents—falls below IRC Section R806.1 net free ventilation area requirements for the attic space, a code deficiency that must be corrected when the roof covering is replaced under the current IL-adopted IRC. The convergence of multi-slope wind damage, non-repairable seal-strip failures, compromised EPDM transition flashing, and mandatory ventilation upgrades establishes that piecemeal repair cannot restore the roof assembly to a code-compliant, weather-tight condition.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Underlayment required over entire roof deck area not covered by ice barrier
code_citation.supplement_argument: Underlayment is code-mandated over the entire roof deck. Areas where ice & water barrier is not installed require felt or synthetic underlayment. Omission does not meet code requirements.
code_citation.title: Underlayment Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Underlayment is code-mandated over the entire roof deck. Areas where ice & water barrier is not installed require felt or synthetic underlayment. Omission does not meet code requirements.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Required at eaves, valleys, and around penetrations per GAF system requirements
manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF warranty VOID without WeatherWatch/StormGuard at eaves and valleys

code_citation.requirement: Ice barrier required at eaves extending from eave edge to at least 24 inches past interior wall line, and in valleys
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.
code_citation.title: Ice Barrier Requirement

line_items.description: Ice & water barrier (2 courses eaves + 1 course valleys)
line_items.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Drip edge shall be provided at eaves and gable rake edges of shingle roofs
code_citation.supplement_argument: Drip edge is required at all eaves and rakes per code. Protects fascia and deck edge from water damage. Not an optional upgrade.
code_citation.title: Drip Edge Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Drip edge required at eaves and rakes per IRC R905.2.7. Minimum 2.5 inches below sheathing, 2 inches up deck. Underlayment OVER drip edge at eaves, UNDER at rakes. 5% waste factor standard

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ventilation required per IRC R806 — 1 SF NFVA per 150 SF attic area (1:150 ratio). OC warranty VOID without proper ventilation. Ridge vent must be R&R during re-roofing

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Step flashing shall be used where roof planes intersect vertical walls
code_citation.supplement_argument: Step flashing is code-required at every wall-to-roof junction. When roofing is replaced, step flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight integrity per code.
code_citation.title: Step Flashing at Wall Junctions

line_items.supplement_argument: Step flashing required at all wall-to-roof intersections per IRC R905.2.8.4. Minimum 10 inches height. Siding removal mandatory for proper installation — nail horizontal flange to roof ONLY, never through siding
Counterflashing required at all roof-to-wall intersections per IRC R905.2.8

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Flashing required at chimney-to-roof intersections including step flashing, counter-flashing, and cricket/saddle where applicable
code_citation.supplement_argument: Chimney flashing is code-required at all chimney-roof intersections. Full replacement includes step flashing, counter-flashing, and apron. Reusing old chimney flashing on new roofing is a code violation.
code_citation.title: Chimney Flashing

line_items.supplement_argument: Chimney flashing is code-required at all chimney-roof intersections. Full replacement includes step flashing, counter-flashing, and apron. Reusing old chimney flashing on new roofing is a code violation.
8 hours roofer labor mandatory on every roofing estimate per industry standard

line_items.supplement_argument: Chimney flashing is code-required at all chimney-roof intersections. Full replacement includes step flashing, counter-flashing, and apron. Reusing old chimney flashing on new roofing is a code violation.

line_items.description: Modified bitumen roofing - 2 ply torch applied

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Underlayment required over entire roof deck area not covered by ice barrier
code_citation.supplement_argument: Underlayment is code-mandated over the entire roof deck. Areas where ice & water barrier is not installed require felt or synthetic underlayment. Omission does not meet code requirements.
code_citation.title: Underlayment Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Underlayment is code-mandated over the entire roof deck. Areas where ice & water barrier is not installed require felt or synthetic underlayment. Omission does not meet code requirements.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Required at eaves, valleys, and around penetrations per GAF system requirements
manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF warranty VOID without WeatherWatch/StormGuard at eaves and valleys

code_citation.requirement: Ice barrier required at eaves extending from eave edge to at least 24 inches past interior wall line, and in valleys
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.
code_citation.title: Ice Barrier Requirement

line_items.description: Ice & water barrier (2 courses eaves + 1 course valleys)
line_items.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Drip edge shall be provided at eaves and gable rake edges of shingle roofs
code_citation.supplement_argument: Drip edge is required at all eaves and rakes per code. Protects fascia and deck edge from water damage. Not an optional upgrade.
code_citation.title: Drip Edge Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Drip edge required at eaves and rakes per IRC R905.2.7. Minimum 2.5 inches below sheathing, 2 inches up deck. Underlayment OVER drip edge at eaves, UNDER at rakes. 5% waste factor standard

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ventilation required per IRC R806 — 1 SF NFVA per 150 SF attic area (1:150 ratio). OC warranty VOID without proper ventilation. Ridge vent must be R&R during re-roofing

code_citation.jurisdiction: Illinois Residential Code (IRC 2021; jurisdictional adoption varies)
code_citation.requirement: Flashing required at chimney-to-roof intersections including step flashing, counter-flashing, and cricket/saddle where applicable
code_citation.supplement_argument: Chimney flashing is code-required at all chimney-roof intersections. Full replacement includes step flashing, counter-flashing, and apron. Reusing old chimney flashing on new roofing is a code violation.
code_citation.title: Chimney Flashing

line_items.supplement_argument: Chimney flashing is code-required at all chimney-roof intersections. Full replacement includes step flashing, counter-flashing, and apron. Reusing old chimney flashing on new roofing is a code violation.

photo_annotations.p03_01: Shingle exposure measured at ~13" from ridge to butt edge; nail line and seal strip visible.
photo_annotations.p03_02: Shingle width measured at ~37" across tab face on truck tailgate.
photo_annotations.p03_03: Shingle end-to-end measures ~7" width; broken seal strip edge visible at separation.
photo_annotations.p04_01: Full Tamko dimensional shingle measured ~40" overall length on truck tailgate.
photo_annotations.p04_02: Valley and ridge junction with chalk marks showing where ice and water barrier is required; multiple wind creases marked along ridge.
photo_annotations.p04_03: Valley junction chalk-marked identifying ice and water barrier replacement areas; wind creases marked with arrows at shingle tabs.
photo_annotations.p05_01: Chalk test square notation on Tamko dimensional shingle documenting wind damage count on this slope section.
photo_annotations.p05_02: Chalk test square with wind damage counts and hail hit tallies documented on slope — notations confirm storm damage pattern.
photo_annotations.p05_03: Wall-to-roof transition shows deteriorated step flashing, moss growth, and wind-displaced shingles at dormer junction.
photo_annotations.p06_01: Wind-lifted shingle tabs at siding junction expose nail line; moss/algae confirms aging but seal breaks are directional wind damage.
photo_annotations.p06_02: EPDM-to-shingle transition flashing exposed; wind-displaced shingle tab reveals metal drip edge — recent mechanical separation.
photo_annotations.p06_03: Back slope test square: W=15+ wind-damaged shingles, H=0 hail hits — wind-dominant damage pattern confirmed.
photo_annotations.p07_01: Valley and ridge junction with chalk-marked identifiers showing where ice and water barrier is required; EPDM transition visible.
photo_annotations.p07_02: West slope test square: W=17 wind-damaged shingles, H=8 hail hits — windward exposure shows heaviest damage.
photo_annotations.p07_03: South slope test square: W=7+ wind-damaged shingles, H=0; chalk X marks individual wind damage locations.
photo_annotations.p08_01: Wind-displaced shingles expose EPDM membrane; GAF Ice & Water Shield staged for emergency temporary repair.
photo_annotations.p08_02: Johnson pitch & slope locator on roof edge measuring roof pitch for replacement material calculation.
photo_annotations.p08_03: B-vent metal flashing base shows deteriorated sealant and rust — leak-vulnerable penetration requiring replacement.
photo_annotations.p09_01: Gutter apron improperly tucked behind gutter; must be detached and reset for proper installation per contractor.
photo_annotations.p09_02: Wind-displaced shingle at EPDM transition exposes metal flashing and fasteners — clean seal break confirms wind event.
photo_annotations.p09_03: Chalk marks at valley junction show where ice and water barrier is required per code.
photo_annotations.p10_01: Chalk-marked X's on right slope identify wind-damaged shingle tabs requiring replacement.
photo_annotations.p10_02: Wind-lifted shingle tab peeled back ~13" from EPDM transition — exposed nail line, no rust confirms recent loss.
photo_annotations.p10_03: Overview of EPDM-to-shingle transition showing wind-displaced shingles along full slope-to-flat junction.
photo_annotations.p11_01: Chalk-marked overhang notation at EPDM-to-shingle transition; wind-displaced shingles expose membrane edge below.
photo_annotations.p11_02: Wind-lifted EPDM membrane edge peeled from drip edge — clean separation confirms recent wind event.
photo_annotations.p11_03: Tape measure documents ~18" EPDM membrane run under shingles at transition; pipe boot flashing visible.
photo_annotations.p12_01: Wider view of EPDM-to-shingle transition with tape measure; chalk slope markings visible on shingles.
photo_annotations.p12_02: Wind-displaced shingle courses lifted off EPDM membrane — open gap exposes decking to water infiltration.
photo_annotations.p12_03: Wind-lifted shingle tab at EPDM transition exposes nail line — no rust on fastener confirms recent storm loss.
photo_annotations.p13_01: Chalk notation '1" OVERHANG' marks where ice and water barrier is required at EPDM-to-shingle transition.
photo_annotations.p13_02: EPDM flat roof overview shows seam weathering and aged membrane; visible seam laps across full section.
photo_annotations.p13_03: Brick chimney at EPDM transition shows severe mortar deterioration, spalling brick, and failing flashing seal.
photo_annotations.p14_01: EPDM-to-shingle valley junction with plumbing vent boot; shingle edge lifting visible at membrane transition.
photo_annotations.p14_02: 1x12 nominal plank decking exposed; tape measure confirms board width — decking visible where shingles wind-displaced.
photo_annotations.p14_03: Wind-displaced shingles at ridge/valley junction expose EPDM membrane and decking beneath.
photo_annotations.p15_01: Wind-lifted shingle tab reveals exposed nail head — no rust confirms recent storm loss.
photo_annotations.p15_02: Wind-lifted shingle tab exposes nail line with clean seal break — functional damage confirmed.
photo_annotations.p15_03: Chalk-marked identifiers at valley junction show where ice and water barrier is required.
photo_annotations.p16_01: Wind-lifted shingle exposing nail line at EPDM transition — rust-free nail confirms recent storm loss.
photo_annotations.p16_02: Wind-lifted shingle tab reveals exposed nail head — no rust confirms recent loss event.
photo_annotations.p16_03: Shingle tab lifted 1"+ off lower shingle exposing nail line — clean seal break confirms wind.
photo_annotations.p17_01: Wind-displaced shingle tabs at EPDM transition with broken seal strips — clean mechanical separation.
photo_annotations.p17_02: Chalk X-marks identify wind-damaged areas; ice and water barrier replacement needed at valley.
photo_annotations.p17_03: Multiple chalk-marked X's document wind-displaced shingles; exposed decking and EPDM membrane visible at center.
photo_annotations.p18_01: Wind-lifted shingle tab with broken seal exposes EPDM membrane and cracked shingle — recent storm loss.
photo_annotations.p18_02: Wind-displaced shingle tab at ridge exposes underlayment; broken seal strip and granule loss at fold line.
photo_annotations.p18_03: TAMKO manufacturer stamp visible on lifted shingle — clean seal break confirms wind uplift, not age.
photo_annotations.p19_01: Chalk-marked hail impact on shingle face with Building Experts Institute gauge measuring ~1" diameter.

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 20 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos show authentic roofing inspection documentation with no fraud indicators detected. Photos demonstrate standard inspection methodology including: chalk test square notations with directional labels and hit counts (wind and hail) on multiple slopes, tape measure documentation of shingle dimensions and exposure, shingle sample collection for manufacturer identification, pitch measurement tool usage, and documentation of roof components (flashing, penetrations, transitions). GPS coordinates and timestamps are consistent across all photos indicating a single inspection event on April 23, 2026, at one property location (38.2125°N, 89.0287°W). The inspection appears to document primarily wind damage (multiple slopes showing elevated wind counts) with some hail damage noted on the west slope (H=8). A temporary tarp/ice and water shield patch suggests active damage requiring protection.

config.user_notes: Tamko dimensional shingle, has 2 box vents on the roof which isn't up to code. Also has 1x12 nominal plank lumber/decking. EPDM rubber runs up under the shingles at minimum 18" currently. a small portion of gutter apron is tucked behind the gutter and the gutter must be detached and reset in order to properly install gutter apron.

noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-10-17] A small cluster of thunderstorms developed and moved all the way across southern Illinois in the early morning. The storms formed just ahead of a strong, sharp 500mb trough in an area of strong upper divergence associated with a small 120kt 300mb jet. Most unstable CAPE was only around 250J/kg, but mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7 C/km and low-level lapse rates were 6-6.5 C/km. Right near the trough line, the shear was negligible. The storms began to produce damaging winds across eastern portions of Perry County and then solidified into a persistent bow echo over Franklin County. It produced damaging winds as it moved northeast through McLeansboro and into White county. | [2025-10-18] A small cluster of thunderstorms developed and moved all the way across southern Illinois in the early morning. The storms formed just ahead of a strong, sharp 500mb trough in an area of strong upper divergence associated with a small 120kt 300mb jet. Most unstable CAPE was only around 250J/kg, but mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7 C/km and low-level lapse rates were 6-6.5 C/km. Right near the trough line, the shear was negligible. The storms began to produce damaging winds across eastern portions of Perry County and then solidified into a persistent bow echo over Franklin County. It produced damaging winds as it moved northeast through McLeansboro and into White county. | [2025-10-19] A small cluster of thunderstorms developed and moved all the way across southern Illinois in the early morning. The storms formed just ahead of a strong, sharp 500mb trough in an area of strong upper divergence associated with a small 120kt 300mb jet. Most unstable CAPE was only around 250J/kg, but mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7 C/km and low-level lapse rates were 6-6.5 C/km. Right near the trough line, the shear was negligible. The storms began to produce damaging winds across eastern portions of Perry County and then solidified into a persistent bow echo over Franklin County. It produced damaging winds as it moved northeast through McLeansboro and into White county. | [2025-10-20] A small cluster of thunderstorms developed and moved all the way across southern Illinois in the early morning. The storms formed just ahead of a strong, sharp 500mb trough in an area of strong upper divergence associated with a small 120kt 300mb jet. Most unstable CAPE was only around 250J/kg, but mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7 C/km and low-level lapse rates were 6-6.5 C/km. Right near the trough line, the shear was negligible. The storms began to produce damaging winds across eastern portions of Perry County and then solidified into a persistent bow echo over Franklin County. It produced damaging winds as it moved northeast through McLeansboro and into White county.
